speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe to me how anti-Jewish laws became progressively more intolerable.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, obviously, for instance, as I mentioned, my brother was chucked out of the Army. All Jews were. And they were put in labor battalions. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then they made it very difficult for Jews to own businesses in their own name. But of course, they tolerated Jews owning businesses under a Christian name. A lot of this was a slap to the Germans who were pressing the Hungarian government to enact more and more stringent anti-Jewish laws. But they weren't really enforced all that intolerantly.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, from the beginning of the war right up to the German occupation in '44. It was tightened all the time. For instance, there were Jewish members of parliament. Now obviously they were chucked out of parliament. And in the end, before the German occupation, even the vote was taken away from Jews. So in actual fact, the Jews ceased to be citizens of Hungary. But nobody took that too seriously. And it had no effect on us, on our daily lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe to me the events when the Arrow Cross took control?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you-- you're a little bit ahead of yourself there because, really, before you get to the Arrow Cross, you would have to speak of the German occupation that made it possible. So we lived a really quite an idyllic existence in Hungary up to March 19, 1944. Or certainly in comparison to Polish Jews, who were being murdered left, right, and center, we were going to the opera, to concerts, to theater, on holidays, as if the war wouldn't have been on. And Hungary at that time was still neutral. And then when the Germans occupied the country, that's when the trouble really started. Very soon, within weeks, Jews were compelled to wear the yellow star, which made movement without false papers impossible. And the next thing was that they pressed Jews into ghettos in all country towns, villages, country towns. And within two months, there was not a single Jew left in the provinces outside of Budapest. Within two months they managed to ship something like 6-- 800,000 Jews to Auschwitz. At that time, there were close to a million Jews in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live when the Germans-- did you still live in [INAUDIBLE] Street when the Germans arrived in Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we still lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] did they try and put the Budapest Jews into ghettos?

speaker: interviewee
text: They never got around to it at that time. They, as a first step, I imagine, they put Jews into Jewish houses, which meant that they designated houses as Jewish, Jewish apartment blocks or Jewish houses. The house received a big yellow star on the outside. And the gate was kept locked all the time. And all Jews had to move there. But oddly, Christians didn't have to move out of these houses. Some remained.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in your apartment block, did that become a Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: That become a Jewish house, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you remained in your--

speaker: interviewee
text: That meant that we didn't have to move at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any other Jews move into your apartment with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there guards on the, on the door of your apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was no guards, but there was a janitor who had to open and shut the door each time somebody came or went. And he wasn't really allowed to allow any, any Jew out at all, except in designated times, a couple of hours a day for shopping or something of that sort.

speaker: interviewer
text: When your house became-- when your apartment became a Jewish block--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what was, what was termed in Hungarian or German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Judenhaus.

speaker: interviewer
text: When it became a Jewish House

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that affect your life? Just as a child, how did, how did that change your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, obviously by then, we could see that the writing was on the wall. And our elders still didn't want to see it. But young people were probably more with it. I don't know how. You see, what you have to remember is that you were brought up to be God-fearing and law-abiding citizens. And all of a sudden, you discover that the law is out to destroy you. And obviously, you have to readjust your thinking. I would think that young kids were much more successful at it than [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] older people, our parents, for instance, or the people who were running the Jewish community. They were all for cooperation with the Germans. And if you cooperate, they'll be reasonable. They won't do us any harm. Well, of course, that was absolutely crazy. Now you ask me how we readjust our thinking. I know that in the house I was trapped. I had to get false papers to be able to move around. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a friend of mine, for instance, he came up with a scheme to go and join the partisans in-- in Yugoslavia. And he managed to get there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, I can't remember his name. He was one class above me. And if you give me a couple of days, then I might come back. But right now, I can't think. I met him after the war and he was happy. He was killing Germans during the war. A great risk, but it was better than being taken to the slaughter. And I knew that I have to be able to get out of there or will be shipped off to Auschwitz, the same as the Jews in the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you know that the Jews in the country went to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, we didn't really know, but you got to remember that you knew what you wanted to know and disregarded what you didn't want to know. And in a way, I am sympathetic to Germans who say they knew nothing about it because they knew, all right, the same as we knew. Now I remember American planes dropping leaflets that 600,000 Jews were gassed in Auschwitz and obviously threatening people-- it was a message from President Roosevelt-- threatening people with war crimes after the war, that they'll be punished after the war, and threatening rewards for those who will help Jews. And I remember my father and I picking up a leaflet and neither of us believed it. And there were a couple of escapees in Budapest from Auschwitz who were brought out before the German occupation by the Jewish community. And nobody really believed their story. I mean, you have to think about it that even today it's difficult to believe. I mean, you know it's there, but that it was there, and it's difficult to believe. I mean for us, it was better not to believe it. And of course, the Germans did everything possible to camouflage it. For instance, a lot of the Jews from the country, relations of ours, sent a postcard from Auschwitz with a postmark named [GERMAN]. Now you looked at the map, [GERMAN] was right next to the Swiss border. And straight after they wrote the card and they said, what a wonderful trip they had, how well they were treated, and all that, they went straight to the gas chambers.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your family get through, after, during the Nazi occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't really remember, but because there was a war on, we had quite a bit of food salted away. People in those days had pantries. There was no shopping as we know it today. You didn't have to go to the shops every day. They had sacks full of sugar, salt and cooking fat and that sort of thing stashed away. The daily shopping, I really don't remember how it was done.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as, as a child, just as in your, your life as a child, when the Nazis came in, how did that change what you did during the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] obviously, when the Germans came, they closed every school, every kindergarten, every university, never to reopen until-- as long as the German occupation lasted. And we were free to cook up all sorts of harebrained schemes to equip ourselves with false papers and this sort of thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a story about your, about getting false papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't really as difficult as it might seem. I don't really remember how I got the papers. I do remember getting birth certificates from the town hall in classmates' names. I do remember getting some chemical to wash out the ink, which is a lot easier than it would seem. And in no time at all, you had a whole set of beautiful false papers. And then you went and-- and got more in a false-- once you had a false name, a false identity, you could accumulate one ID card after another. For instance, I went and did a course at the ambulance service. They gave you a beautiful piece of paper with my false name on it, with a beautiful rubber stamp that was accepted anywhere. It wasn't difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name on these false papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Furthos. That's a good ancient-sounding Hungary name, and not too far away from my own name.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were-- the false papers identified you as a-- as non-Jew. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. You had to learn the catechism by heart, you know. I knew it by heart for the Catholic catechism.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it allow you to do, these false papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: You could do whatever you liked. You could get around, you could go to the pictures, you could sit on a park bench. I mean, by then, a Jew couldn't go to a hairdresser or sit on a park bench.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did most of your friends have false papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my, [INTERPOSING VOICES] my brother was in Russia. But my parents were still living under the illusion that, if we play ball with them, they'll, they'll treat us OK. No, I didn't believe that anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have Schutz-passes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, yes. Now that came much later in the piece, that came after the Arrow Cross takeover. When the Arrow Cross took over, now that was in October 1944, you would have thought that the idiots would have known that the war was lost. But then again, look, the way they believed in the wonder weapons coming and, and beating the Allies, I mean, that, that was really, really touching in its stupidity. But that's when they started to establish a ghetto in October, November 1944. I mean, you've got to remember the war finished within six months. And in Budapest, they had probably three months before the Russians came. They started to build the ghetto in the vicinity where orthodox Jewry in Budapest lived. And it was a kind of a temporary affair with wooden, with timber walls-- but nevertheless, a ghetto, OK. And then they started-- the Hungarian police started escorting Jews from the Jewish houses into the ghetto day after day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about--

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's when they set up the international ghetto. That was your original question to-- the neutral powers tried to save as many Jews as they possibly could. Now Wallenberg today is given a lot of credit. In fact, he's given all the credit what all the neutral powers did. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They did their best. It wasn't worth a lot. But nevertheless, it was, it was a great help.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe to me what happened to your household after they already crossed over, after your apartment block?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as luck would have it, they designated it as Swiss, into a Swiss-protected house. And at the beginning, Swiss protection was OK. And of course, it may be possible for us to stay, provided I managed to get a Swiss Schutz-pass. And I got one, a forged one, again. But nevertheless, it looked all right. And it worked. The Gestapo came checking one day, and they accepted it. Now the next time-- because the Gestapo were around all the time, you know, they wouldn't leave these houses alone. And the next time they came, I knew it wouldn't pass because by then there were thousands of Swiss Schutz-passes around. And I knew that they would come with a list, which they did. And they virtually emptied the house out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents have Swiss Schutz-passes as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, yes. But my father at that time was already Auschwitz. The Gestapo came for him and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sent him to Vienna and eventually to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about what happened when your father got taken away by the Gestapo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what happened was that my father ran his business [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] under the name of one of his employees. And this employee, his name was [HUNGARIAN]. He went in partnership with the Gestapo, he had a lot of his Jewish friends, pointed them out. The Gestapo obligingly came and took them, deported them to Auschwitz and they divided the loot. In the case of my father, of course, he had the added incentive that when my father was gone, he was the official owner of the businesses. He was OK. And so the Gestapo came and took my father. He told me to leave because then the situation was absolutely chaotic. There was fighting in the streets, air raids, there was fighting between the Arrow Cross and the Hungarian Army. The Germans were shooting. Some misguided people took pot shots at the Germans, in working class districts, mainly. And I still remember a German Tiger tank-- in fact, I can see it as clear as anything-- standing at the National Theater. They reduced whole streets to rubble. And I remember that huge tank, the size of a house. Each time it fired a shot, it jumped in the air. And in between times, there were air raids and murder in the streets. It was indescribable. So my father said, a friend of his would put me up. I should go and see him, leave the long way from us. And it took me even two or three times the actual length of the distance to get there. I had to cross the Danube twice to get there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the suburb?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where he lived-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't remember the name of the suburb now. I do remember the name of the street-- [HUNGARIAN], it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the person?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, yes. His name was Joseph Kalman. A very decent old guy of a-- he probably wasn't even old. To me he seemed old. A contemporary of my father, they served their apprenticeship together. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he opened the door when I knocked on it. And, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and I said to him, my father sent me and my father's name. And he put his arm around me and he said, my dear son, come in, stay as long as you'd like. I mean, that, that was really indescribable.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay there for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I started there about two weeks, I'd say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody else stay with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it an apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did he conceal from the other people in the block that you were staying with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, obviously, I couldn't go to the air raid shelter with him. Budapest had a very well-developed system of air raid shelters. Look, I had false papers, but obviously he didn't want to risk it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in those few days, not many questions were asked, you know. If they were suspicious, they shot you like a dog. The Arrow Cross would do absolutely anything. The Germans were even polite about murder, you know. These, these, I can't say that they were people, they were homicidal maniacs, you know. They were absolutely crazy. And they didn't need much excuse at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed in the flat. Since I couldn't go down, the old bloke wouldn't go down either. But I do remember that his son came occasionally. And I had to stay in another room because his son was a member of the Arrow Cross. And did you hear any conversations between them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they talk about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I heard heated conversations. And I can't remember details. But obviously they were discussing politics and they didn't agree. And if the son would have known that his father was hiding a Jew, well, without a doubt, that would have been the end of the road, possibly for both of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: When, when you left this person's apartment, did you return home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back to my mother, who was still in, in that house?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know it was safe to leave the apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: You didn't know anything. But look, the [INAUDIBLE] telegraph was surprisingly efficient. You got an awful lot of news without ever reading a newspaper. The newspapers were useless, anyway. And it was safer by then, the situation settled down a bit. And I barely got home, my mother started crying that I ought to go because they called up every Jew between the ages of 14 and probably 60, 65, I can't remember, to-- they drafted every Jew for labor. And my mother was begging me that I should go because, of course, it was on pain of death if you didn't. And against my better judgment, I went. And I didn't stay very long. I stayed probably a fortnight.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother tell you what happened to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did your father go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I found all this out, of course, much later because there was no way of checking it. My father was taken to Buda, which is the other side of the Danube, and kept in a, in a Gestapo prison for a few days. Then they sent him to Vienna. And from Vienna, on his own, with a guard, they sent him to Auschwitz. I often thought that's the reason why the Germans lost the war. Every single Jew mattered.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your mother tell you about when Gestapo came to take away your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a lot. One thing that sticks in my mind-- shortly before they came, my father had a revolver and he dug it in a hole in the garden. And she thought a good thing they didn't find it on him, indeed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why didn't they take your mother away? You don't know why didn't?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you mentioned that you went into a labor group.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in charge of this labor group?

speaker: interviewee
text: The army.

speaker: interviewer
text: The army.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was thought at the time that the army arranged all of that to protect the Jews. It may or may not be true. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of the uniforms did the officers wear?

speaker: interviewee
text: The officers wore army uniform. And you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: These are Hungarian Army uniforms?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hungarian Army. And it did happen, the Arrow Cross were always hovering on, on the periphery. If they could grab a Jew and kill him, they would. And I do remember there was a lieutenant in charge of hauling the Arrow Cross over the coals for killing one boy, because he didn't shave, believe it or not, that day. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the army was not in a strong position against the Arrow Cross. Both were armed, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you notice a type of disagreement between the Arrow Cross and the regular army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the regular army trying? Did you see them actually try and save any Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't say save, but protect the Jews under their command.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your labor battalion working?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in a place called [HUNGARIAN], which oddly is the site of the International Air Terminal in Budapest now. At that time, it was just wide open spaces.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you describe a day, just what would happen in a regular day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were digging tank ditches. And they were awfully deep, that I remember, and a lot of digging out actually. It was just spade and shovel work. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I do remember unloading railway trucks, wheat from railway trucks. That's about all. We weren't there very long. At night, I do remember meeting a lot of people who we knew, among others-- for instance, my headmaster, as well as teachers from our school. Just about everybody was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any other incidences that occurred while you were in labor battalion?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, none I remember there. The one thing that sticks in my memory that, when the Russians were so close that we could see Russian things, all of a sudden gendarmes came out of nowhere and [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] told us to assemble and take us, they were going to take us elsewhere. And these gendarmes had a bad reputation. They were responsible for all the deportation of Jews to Auschwitz, incidentally. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were-- it was one night, they got us, got us marching in sort of rough units. And the one incident I remember was that they wouldn't carry their own ammunition. They had Tommy guns and a kind of a round box full of ammunition, which was damn heavy. And we had our own rucksacks to carry. So they gave their ammunition box to the nearest Jew in the formation, who was handing it over after the-- after a while to the next one, until it came to me, and I threw the damn thing away. I must have been either stupid or a hero. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And nothing ever came of it. They were looking for it, they couldn't find it. Nothing happened. And they were in a hurry to get to the other side of the Danube because, obviously, they felt that the Russians wouldn't stop.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of the other prisoners around you say anything to you when you threw it away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, I can't remember if I would have told anybody. I probably didn't. Because if I would have told anybody, they would have told me not to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the month that you were in the labor battalion.

speaker: interviewee
text: It would have been end of October or beginning of November.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Perhaps November.
